text: Laparoscopic adenolactomy is increasingly popular technique for surgical removal of adrenal gland and it is as safe and effective as open adrenalectomy. Contrary to conventional thinking, laparoscopy may outscore the open approach in terms of magnification and identification of ambiguous plans once the learning curve has been transcended. Here we present a case where ambiguous plans of adrenal with IVC, though a challenge in open technique, turn out to be a good dissection with the available magnification of laparoscopy. A forty-year-old female with history of hypertension and hypothyroidism presented with right flank pain. Her BP was hundred fifty-six by ninety-four millimeter mercury on medications.

USG showed right adrenal mass with small atrophic right kidney. Plasma-free metanephrines were thirty-four picograms per milliliter and plasma-free non-metanephrines were three thousand nine hundred ninety-eight picograms per milliliter. CCT abdomen showed a large lobulated heterogeneous enhancing mass in right adrenal region with loss of fat plans between mass and upper pole of right kidney, inferior surface of right lobe of liver, and right psoas muscle. Right kidney was small in size and non-functioning. The main and accessory renal artery was seen through the lesion and supplied. There was doubt of adrenal invasion in posterior wall of IVC. Patient was preoperatively optimized and underwent right adrenalectomy with N-block nephrectomy.

The patient is positioned in the right flank position in the operating room. An 11 mm camera port is placed, along with 5 mm working ports in the right iliac fossa and a 10 mm port just below the costal line. A 5 mm port for liver retraction is positioned below the xiphoid process. After dissecting the ureter free, another 5 mm working port is placed 2 cm above and medial to the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS). The mass is visibly adherent to the right kidney, renal vein, and renal artery. The colon is dissected down from its peritoneal attachments.

The surgeon performs limited dissection of the upper pole to remove attachments, followed by medial mobilization of the colon. A window is created at the lateral fold of the peritoneum, uplifting the ureter and gonadal vein. Dissection continues upward to the renal hilum, with the lower pole freed from surrounding tissues. Careful dissection is conducted at the adrenal hilum, where the adrenal gland is mobilized superiorly, and the adrenal vein is identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the mass from the undersurface of the liver. The renal artery is clipped with Hem-o-lok clips and divided. Dense adhesions surrounding the renal vein are removed, and the renal vein is divided between clips. The ureter is clipped and divided. The specimen is placed into a bag and removed through a Pfannenstiel incision.

The surgeon secures complete hemostasis and places an abdominal drain before closing the surgical site. Despite thorough dissection of the adrenal mass, the patient's blood pressure remained elevated during surgery but dropped to subnormal levels after clipping the renal vein.

and twelve twenty milliliter on post-operative day two drain was removed on post-operative day two on follow-up after two weeks patient had normal plasma metanephrines and non-metanephrines her bp was hundred forty by seventy millimeter mercury without antihypertensives to conclude increasingly popular laparoscopy may actually turn to be superior modality for discerning plans under magnification over and above open technique thank you
